items.q: Are 'independent events' and 'mutually exclusive events' the same?
items.correct: No — independent events do not affect each other's probability; mutually exclusive events cannot both happen.
items.misconception: Yes — both mean the events are unrelated.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only when the probabilities are equal.
items.why: Students learn these terms close together and conflate them; in fact mutually exclusive non-zero events are never independent.

items.correct: 4.6 — look at the second decimal (4), which is less than 5, so round down.
items.misconception: 4.7, because the 8 at the end rounds the 4 up to 5, which then rounds the 6 up to 7.
items.random_wrong: 4.65, because rounding to 1 dp means cutting after the second decimal.
items.why: Students apply 'rounding up' digit-by-digit from the right (chained rounding), instead of looking only at the next digit after the place to which they are rounding.

items.q: On a distance-time graph, what does a horizontal line mean?
items.correct: The object is stationary — distance is not changing, so speed is zero.
items.misconception: The object is moving at a constant speed.
items.why: Students confuse distance-time graphs with speed-time graphs; a horizontal line on the latter would mean constant speed.

items.q: Using Newton-Raphson on f(x) = x² − 2 with x₀ = 1.5, what is x₁? (Give to 3 dp.)
items.why: It's 1.417. “1.500” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The trapezium rule estimates which kind of quantity?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: a definite integral. “a derivative” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Use trapezium rule with 2 strips to estimate ∫₀² x² dx (h = 1).
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
